sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 40, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: RIGHT moves black F right + dark gray F left; LEFT moves black F left + dark gray F right. When they overlap, dark gray F parts get consumed. Currently dark gray F has only its vertical bar (18 pixels) at cols 27-29, while black F's vertical bar is at cols 33-35. Need to align them to consume the remaining dark gray F.
- Goal model: Need to figure out the target configuration. The black F has 5 white dots that may indicate a target pattern or position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 39 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT from state 18 led to state 19. Let me check what changed:
- Dark gray F: pixels=36 (was 45), boundary=[[18, 24], [18, 29], [20, 29], [20, 27], [21, 26], [26, 26], [26, 24]]
- Black F: pixels=40, boundary=[[18, 30], [18, 38], [26, 38], [26, 36], [21, 36], [20, 35], [20, 30]]
- White dots: (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37) - only 4 white dots now (was 5)
- One white dot disappeared: (19, 40) is gone
So when the black F and dark gray F overlap, the dark gray F is partially consumed (pixels=36, lost 9 pixels from the top bar), and one white dot disappeared.
The white dot at (19, 40) was on the right side of the black F. When the black F moved left, this white dot might have overlapped with the dark gray F and was consumed.
Wait, but the white dots are on the black F, not on the dark gray F. So the white dots should move with the black F.
Let me re-examine. At state 18, the white dots were at (19, 34), (19, 37), (19, 40), (22, 40), (25, 40). At state 19, the white dots are at (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37).
The white dots at (19, 40) and (22, 40), (25, 40) changed to (22, 37), (25, 37). Wait, that doesn't make sense. The white dots should move with the black F.
Actually, let me re-examine. At state 18, the black F was at cols 33-41. The white dots were at cols 34, 37, 40.
At state 19, the black F is at cols 30-38. The white dots are at cols 34, 37, 37, 37.
Wait, the white dots at state 19 are at (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37). This is different from state 18's (19, 34), (19, 37), (19, 40), (22, 40), (25, 40).
So the white dots changed:
- (19, 40) -> disappeared
- (22, 40) -> (22, 37)
- (25, 40) -> (25, 37)
This suggests that the white dots are not fixed to the black F's shape. They change based on the overlap with the dark gray F.
Wait, but (19, 34) and (19, 37) remained the same. So some white dots are stable and others change.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. The white dots might indicate the parts of the dark gray F that have been consumed. When the black F overlaps with the dark gray F, the overlapping parts of the dark gray F are consumed, and the white dots indicate which parts were consumed.
At state 19, the dark gray F has pixels=36 (lost 9 pixels from the top bar). The white dots are at (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37).
Wait, but the white dots are on the black F, not on the dark gray F. So the white dots can't directly indicate the consumed parts of the dark gray F.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the white dots indicate the target configuration for the dark gray F. The goal is to consume the dark gray F such that only the parts corresponding to the white dots remain.
At state 19, the dark gray F has pixels=36. The white dots are at (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37).
The dark gray F's boundary is [[18, 24], [18, 29], [20, 29], [20, 27], [21, 26], [26, 26], [26, 24]]. This describes:
- Top bar: rows 18-20, cols 24-29 (3 rows x 6 cols = 18 pixels)
- Vertical bar: rows 21-26, cols 24-26 (6 rows x 3 cols = 18 pixels)
Total: 36 pixels. So the dark gray F's top bar was reduced from 9 cols to 6 cols (cols 21-23 were consumed).
The white dots at (19, 34), (19, 37), (22, 37), (25, 37) are on the black F, not on the dark gray F. So they don't directly correspond to the consumed parts.
I'm getting confused. Let me try a different approach. Let me check if pressing LEFT again from state 19 leads to further consumption.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: LEFT from state 18 led to state 19. Dark gray F consumed (pixels=36), one white dot disappeared. The white dots seem to change based on overlap. Let me try LEFT again to see if more consumption happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT again from state 19
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"LEFT result: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}, no_impact={result.get('no_impact')}, done={result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['B', 'c', 'Y', 'W'] and n['pixels'] <= 100:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print(f"\nCurrent state: {state_graph.current}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
